And wherever you ňĻbelieversňļ are, face towards it, so that people will have no argument against you, except the wrongdoers among them. Do not fear them; fear Me, so that I may ňĻcontinue toňļ perfect My favour upon you and so you may be ňĻrightlyňļ guided.

2:151.text: Since We have sent you a messenger from among yourselves‚ÄĒreciting to you Our revelations, purifying you, teaching you the Book and wisdom, and teaching you what you never knew‚ÄĒ

2:152.text: remember Me; I will remember you. And thank Me, and never be ungrateful.

2:153.text: O believers! Seek comfort in patience and prayer. Allah is truly with those who are patient.

2:154.text: Never say that those martyred in the cause of Allah are dead‚ÄĒin fact, they are alive! But you do not perceive it.

2:155.text: We will certainly test you with a touch of fear and famine and loss of property, life, and crops. Give good news to those who patiently endure‚ÄĒ

2:156.text: who say, when struck by a disaster, ‚ÄúSurely to Allah we belong and to Him we will ňĻallňļ return.‚ÄĚ

2:157.text: They are the ones who will receive Allah‚Äôs blessings and mercy. And it is they who are ňĻrightlyňļ guided.

2:158.text: Indeed, ňĻthe hills ofňļ ŠĻĘafa¬†and Marwah<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†are among the symbols of Allah. So whoever makes the major or minor pilgrimage to the ňĻSacredňļ House,<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†let them walk between ňĻthe two hillsňļ. And whoever does good willingly, Allah is truly Appreciative, All-Knowing.
footnotes: ¬†ŠĻĘafa and Marwah¬†are two hills near the Ka‚Äôbah in Mecca.
¬†Known as Šł•ajj¬†and ‚Äôumrah¬†to the Sacred Mosque in Mecca.

2:159.text: Those who hide the clear proofs and guidance that We have revealed‚ÄĒafter We made it clear for humanity in the Book‚ÄĒwill be condemned by Allah and ňĻallňļ those who condemn.

2:160.text: As for those who repent, mend their ways, and let the truth be known, they are the ones to whom I will turn ňĻin forgivenessňļ, for I am the Accepter of Repentance, Most Merciful.

2:161.text: Surely those who disbelieve and die as disbelievers are condemned by Allah, the angels, and all of humanity.

2:162.text: They will be in Hell forever. Their punishment will not be lightened, nor will they be delayed ňĻfrom itňļ.

2:163.text: Your God is ňĻonlyňļ One God. There is no god ňĻworthy of worshipňļ except Him‚ÄĒthe Most Compassionate, Most Merciful.

2:164.text: Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth; the alternation of the day and the night; the ships that sail the sea for the benefit of humanity; the rain sent down by Allah from the skies, reviving the earth after its death; the scattering of all kinds of creatures throughout; the shifting of the winds; and the clouds drifting between the heavens and the earth‚ÄĒňĻin all of thisňļ are surely signs for people of understanding.

2:165.text: Still there are some who take others as Allah‚Äôs equal‚ÄĒthey love them as they should love Allah‚ÄĒbut the ňĻtrueňļ believers love Allah even more. If only the wrongdoers could see the ňĻhorribleňļ punishment ňĻawaiting themňļ, they would certainly realize that all power belongs to Allah and that Allah is indeed severe in punishment.

2:166.text: ňĻConsider the Dayňļ when those who misled others will disown their followers‚ÄĒwhen they face the torment‚ÄĒand the bonds that united them will be cut off.

2:167.text: The ňĻmisledňļ followers will cry, ‚ÄúIf only we could have a second chance, we would disown them as they disowned us.‚ÄĚ And so Allah will make them remorseful of their misdeeds. And they will never ňĻbe able toňļ leave the Fire.

2:168.text: O humanity! Eat from what is lawful and good on the earth and do not follow Satan‚Äôs footsteps. He is truly your sworn enemy.

2:169.text: He only incites you to commit evil and indecency, and to claim against Allah what you do not know.

2:170.text: When it is said to them, ‚ÄúFollow what Allah has revealed,‚ÄĚ they reply, ‚ÄúNo! We ňĻonlyňļ follow what we found our forefathers practicing.‚ÄĚ ňĻWould they still do so,ňļ even if their forefathers had ňĻabsolutelyňļ no understanding or guidance?

2:171.text: The example of the disbelievers ňĻnot responding to the Messenger‚Äôs warningňļ is like a flock not comprehending the calls and cries of the shepherd. ňĻThey are wilfullyňļ deaf, dumb and blind so they have no understanding.

2:172.text: O believers! Eat from the good things We have provided for you. And give thanks to Allah if you ňĻtrulyňļ worship Him ňĻaloneňļ.

2:173.text: He has only forbidden you ňĻto eatňļ carrion, blood, swine,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†and what is slaughtered in the name of any other than Allah. But if someone is compelled by necessity‚ÄĒneither driven by desire nor exceeding immediate need‚ÄĒthey will not be sinful. Surely Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.
footnotes: ¬†Eating pork is forbidden in the Old Testament in Leviticus 11:7-8 and Deuteronomy 14:8.

2:174.text: Indeed, those who hide Allah‚Äôs revelations, trading them for a fleeting gain consume nothing but fire into their bellies. Allah will neither speak to them on the Day of Judgment, nor will He purify them. And they will suffer a painful punishment.

2:175.text: They are the ones who trade guidance for misguidance and forgiveness for punishment. How persistent are they in pursuit of the Fire!

2:176.text: That is because Allah has revealed the Book in truth. And surely those who differ regarding it are totally engrossed in opposition.

2:177.text: Righteousness is not in turning your faces towards the east or the west. Rather, the righteous are those who believe in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Books, and the prophets; who give charity out of their cherished wealth to relatives, orphans, the poor, ňĻneedyňļ travellers, beggars, and for freeing captives; who establish prayer, pay alms-tax, and keep the pledges they make; and who are patient in times of suffering, adversity, and in ňĻthe heat ofňļ battle. It is they who are true ňĻin faithňļ, and it is they who are mindful ňĻof Allahňļ.

2:178.text: O believers! ňĻThe law ofňļ retaliation is set for you in cases of murder‚ÄĒa free man for a free man, a slave for a slave, and a female for a female.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†But if the offender is pardoned by the victim‚Äôs guardian,<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†then blood-money should be decided fairly<sup foot_note=3>3</sup>¬†and payment should be made courteously. This is a concession and a mercy from your Lord. But whoever transgresses after that will suffer a painful punishment.
footnotes: ¬†No one else should be executed in place of the killer. This verse was revealed regarding a particular incident. Nevertheless, the killer is killed regardless of the difference in gender or status, unless the victim‚Äôs family opts for blood money.
¬†The guardian is the victim‚Äôs closest heir‚ÄĒmale or female.
¬†Or according to common law.

2:179.text: There is ňĻsecurity ofňļ life for you in ňĻthe law ofňļ retaliation, O ¬†people of reason, so that you may become mindful ňĻof Allahňļ.

2:180.text: It is prescribed that when death approaches any of you‚ÄĒif they leave something of value‚ÄĒa will should be made in favour of parents and immediate family with fairness.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†ňĻThis isňļ an obligation on those who are mindful ňĻof Allahňļ.
footnotes: ¬†This ruling should be understood in light of the inheritance laws in 4:11-12, which give specific shares to parents and close relatives. Relatives who do not have a share may get a bequest up to one third of the estate.

2:181.text: But whoever changes the will after hearing it,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†the blame will only be on those who made the change. Indeed, Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.
footnotes: ¬†After the death of the one who made the will.

2:182.text: Whoever suspects an error or an injustice in the will and brings about a ňĻfairňļ settlement among the parties will not be sinful. Surely Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.

2:183.text: O believers! Fasting is prescribed for you‚ÄĒas it was for those before you<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>‚ÄĒso perhaps you will become mindful ňĻof Allahňļ.
footnotes: ¬†i.e., the followers of previous prophets.

2:184.text: ňĻFast aňļ prescribed number of days.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†But whoever of you is ill or on a journey, then ňĻlet them fastňļ an equal number of days ňĻafter RamaŠłć√Ęnňļ. For those who can only fast with extreme difficulty,<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†compensation can be made by feeding a needy person ňĻfor every day not fastedňļ. But whoever volunteers to give more, it is better for them. And to fast is better for you, if only you knew.
footnotes: ¬†i.e., the month of RamaŠłć√Ęn, the 9th¬†month in the Islamic calendar.
¬†In the case of old age or chronic disease.

2:185.text: RamaŠłć√Ęn is the month in which the Quran was revealed as a guide for humanity with clear proofs of guidance and the decisive authority. So whoever is present this month, let them fast. But whoever is ill or on a journey, then ňĻlet them fastňļ an equal number of days ňĻafter RamaŠłć√Ęnňļ. Allah intends ease for you, not hardship, so that you may complete the prescribed period and proclaim the greatness of Allah for guiding you, and perhaps you will be grateful.

2:186.text: When My servants ask you ňĻO Prophetňļ about Me: I am truly near. I respond to one‚Äôs prayer when they call upon Me. So let them respond ňĻwith obedienceňļ to Me and believe in Me, perhaps they will be guided ňĻto the Right Wayňļ.

2:187.text: It has been made permissible for you to be intimate with your wives during the nights preceding the fast. Your spouses are a garment<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†for you as you are for them. Allah knows that you were deceiving yourselves.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†So He has accepted your repentance and pardoned you. So now you may be intimate with them and seek what Allah has prescribed for you.<sup foot_note=3>3</sup>¬†ňĻYou mayňļ eat and drink until you see the light of dawn breaking the darkness of night, then complete the fast until nightfall. Do not be intimate with your spouses while you are meditating in the mosques. These are the limits set by Allah, so do not exceed them. This is how Allah makes His revelations clear to people, so they may become mindful ňĻof Himňļ.
footnotes: ¬†‚ÄúGarment‚ÄĚ (lib√Ęs) is a metaphor for comfort, chastity, and protection.

¬†Initially, intimate relations during the night of RamaŠłć√Ęn were not permissible. Since some Muslims could not resist not having intercourse with their spouses, this verse was revealed allowing intimacy during the nights preceding the fast.
¬†This implies offspring.

2:188.text: Do not consume one another‚Äôs wealth unjustly, nor deliberately bribe authorities in order to devour a portion of others‚Äô property, knowing that it is a sin.

2:189.text: They ask you ňĻO Prophetňļ about the phases of the moon. Say, ‚ÄúThey are a means for people to determine time and pilgrimage.‚ÄĚ Righteousness is not in entering your houses from the back doors.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Rather, righteousness is to be mindful ňĻof Allahňļ. So enter your homes through their ňĻproperňļ doors, and be mindful of Allah so you may be successful.
footnotes: ¬†Before Islam, there was a superstitious practice for pilgrims to enter their homes through the back door upon returning from the pilgrimage. The verse suggests that being devoted to Allah wholeheartedly is more important than blindly following old traditions.

2:190.text: Fight in the cause of Allah ňĻonlyňļ against those who wage war against you, but do not exceed the limits.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Allah does not like transgressors.
footnotes: ¬†‚ÄúDo not exceed the limits‚ÄĚ refers to Islamic warfare guidelines set by the Prophet. In an authentic saying collected by Abu Daw√Ľd, he (Ô∑ļ) is reported to have instructed the Muslim army, ‚ÄúDepart in the Name of Allah and with His help‚ÄĒfollowing the way of the Messenger of Allah. Do not kill an old man, a child, or a woman. Do not mutilate dead bodies of the enemy. Be gracious and courteous, for Allah loves those who act with grace.‚ÄĚ The Prophet (Ô∑ļ) also says, ‚ÄúDo not wish to meet your enemy in battle but always pray for well-being. If fighting is a must, then be steadfast.‚ÄĚ

2:191.text: Kill them wherever you come upon them<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†and drive them out of the places from which they have driven you out. For persecution<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†is far worse than killing. And do not fight them at the Sacred Mosque unless they attack you there. If they do so, then fight them‚ÄĒthat is the reward of the disbelievers.
footnotes: ¬†This is probably the most misquoted verse from the Quran. To properly understand the verse, we need to put it into context. Misquoting verses and taking them out of context can be applied to any scripture. For example, in the Bible, Jesus says, ‚ÄúDo not think that I came to bring peace on earth. I did not come to bring peace but a sword!‚ÄĚ (Matthew 10:34). He also says, ‚ÄúBut as for these enemies of mine who did not want me to be their king‚ÄĒbring them here and slaughter them in my presence!‚ÄĚ (Luke 19:27). And Moses says, ‚ÄúThe Lord is a man of war, the Lord is his name.‚ÄĚ (Exodus 15:3). For more details, see¬†the Introduction.
¬†Persecuting Muslims to abandon their faith.

2:192.text: But if they cease, then surely Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.

2:193.text: Fight against them ňĻif they persecute youňļ until there is no more persecution, and ňĻyourňļ devotion will be to Allah ňĻaloneňļ. If they stop ňĻpersecuting youňļ, let there be no hostility except against the aggressors.

2:194.text: ňĻThere will be retaliation inňļ a sacred month for ňĻan offence inňļ a sacred month,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†and all violations will bring about retaliation. So, if anyone attacks you, retaliate in the same manner. ňĻButňļ be mindful of Allah, and know that Allah is with those mindful ňĻof Himňļ.
footnotes: ¬†The sacred months are the 1st, 7th, 11th, and 12th¬†months of the Islamic calendar.

2:195.text: Spend in the cause of Allah and do not let your own hands throw you into destruction ňĻby withholdingňļ. And do good, for Allah certainly loves the good-doers.

2:196.text: Complete the pilgrimage and minor pilgrimage for Allah.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†But if prevented ňĻfrom proceedingňļ, then ňĻofferňļ whatever sacrificial animals you can afford. And do not shave your heads until the sacrificial animal reaches its destination. But if any of you is ill or has a scalp ailment ňĻrequiring shavingňļ, then compensate either by fasting, charity, or a sacrificial offering. In times of peace, you may combine the pilgrimage and minor pilgrimage then make the sacrificial offering you can afford. Whoever cannot afford that ňĻofferingňļ, let them fast three days during pilgrimage and seven after returning ňĻhomeňļ‚ÄĒcompleting ten. These offerings are for those who do not live near the Sacred House. And be mindful of Allah, and know that Allah is severe in punishment.
footnotes: ¬†Šł§ajj,¬†the pilgrimage to Mecca, is required at least once in a Muslim‚Äôs lifetime if they are physically and financially able to. ‚ÄôUmrah,¬†which is a short version of Šł•ajj, is recommended, but not obligatory.

2:197.text: ňĻCommitment toňļ pilgrimage is made in appointed months.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Whoever commits to ňĻperformingňļ pilgrimage, let them stay away from intimate relations, foul language, and arguments during pilgrimage. Whatever good you do, Allah ňĻfullyňļ knows of it. Take ňĻnecessaryňļ provisions ňĻfor the journeyňļ‚ÄĒsurely the best provision is righteousness. And be mindful of Me, O ¬†people of reason!
footnotes: ¬†Although pilgrimage is performed over the course of a few days in the 12th¬†month of the Islamic calendar, the intention to perform pilgrimage can be made during the 10th, 11th, and the first half of the 12th¬†months.

2:198.text: There is no blame on you for seeking the bounty of your Lord ňĻduring this journeyňļ.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†When you return from ‚ÄôAraf√Ęt,<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†praise Allah near the sacred place<sup foot_note=3>3</sup>¬†and praise Him for having guided you, for surely before this ňĻguidanceňļ you were astray.
footnotes: ¬†By trading during Šł•ajj¬†season.
¬†‚ÄôAraf√Ęt is a hill in Saudi Arabia, near Mecca, which is visited during pilgrimage.
¬†A sacred place called Muzdalifah.

2:199.text: Then go forth with the rest of the pilgrims. And seek Allah‚Äôs forgiveness. Surely Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.

2:200.text: When you have fulfilled your sacred rites, praise Allah as you used to praise your forefathers ňĻbefore Islamňļ, or even more passionately. There are some who say, ‚ÄúOur Lord! Grant us ňĻYour bountiesňļ in this world,‚ÄĚ but they will have no share in the Hereafter.

2:201.text: Yet there are others who say, ‚ÄúOur Lord! Grant us the good of this world and the Hereafter, and protect us from the torment of the Fire.‚ÄĚ

2:202.text: It is they who will receive a ňĻheavenlyňļ reward for the good they have done. And Allah is swift in reckoning.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†
footnotes: ¬†Swift in reckoning (sar√ģ‚Äôul-Šł•is√Ęb) means ‚ÄúHe is quick in recording the deeds and judging them.‚ÄĚ

2:203.text: And remember Allah during ňĻtheseňļ appointed days.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Whoever departs swiftly on the second day is not sinful, neither are those who stay behind ňĻtill the third‚ÄĒseeking additional rewardňļ, so long as they are mindful ňĻof their Lordňļ. And be mindful of Allah, and know that to Him you will ňĻallňļ be gathered.
footnotes: ¬†The 11th-13th¬†of ‚Īęul-Šł§ijjah, the 12th¬†month of the Islamic calendar. These days are part of pilgrimage rituals.

2:204.text: There are some ňĻhypocritesňļ who impress you with their views regarding worldly affairs and openly call upon Allah to witness what is in their hearts, yet they are your worst adversaries.

2:205.text: And when they leave ňĻyouňļ,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†they strive throughout the land to spread mischief in it and destroy crops and cattle. Allah does not like mischief.
footnotes: ¬†Another possible translation: ‚ÄúAnd when they assume authority, they strive ‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ

2:206.text: When it is said to them, ‚ÄúFear Allah,‚ÄĚ pride carries them off to sin. Hell will be their proper place. What an evil place to rest!

2:207.text: And there are those who would dedicate their lives to Allah‚Äôs pleasure. And Allah is Ever Gracious to ňĻHisňļ servants.

2:208.text: O believers! Enter into Islam wholeheartedly and do not follow Satan‚Äôs footsteps. Surely he is your sworn enemy.

2:209.text: If you falter after receiving the clear proofs, then know that Allah is indeed Almighty, All-Wise.

2:210.text: Are they waiting for Allah ňĻHimselfňļ to come to them in the shade of clouds, along with the angels? ňĻIf He didňļ, then the matter would be settled ňĻat onceňļ. And to Allah ňĻallňļ matters will be returned ňĻfor judgmentňļ.

2:211.text: Ask the Children of Israel how many clear signs We have given them. And whoever trades Allah‚Äôs favour‚ÄĒafter receiving it‚ÄĒňĻfor disbeliefňļ should know that Allah is indeed severe in punishment.

2:212.text: The life of this world has been made appealing to the disbelievers, and they mock the believers. Those who are mindful ňĻof Allahňļ will rank above them on the Day of Judgment. And Allah provides for whoever He wills without limit.

2:213.text: Humanity had once been one community ňĻof believers before they lost faithňļ. Then Allah raised prophets as deliverers of good news and as warners, and revealed to them the Scriptures in truth to judge among people regarding their disputes. And no one disputed the Scriptures except the very people who received them after clear proofs had come to them‚ÄĒout of jealousy. Then Allah, by His grace, has guided the believers to the truth regarding those disputes. And Allah guides whoever He wills to the Straight Path.

2:214.text: Do you think you will be admitted into Paradise without being tested like those before you? They were afflicted with suffering and adversity and were so ňĻviolentlyňļ shaken that ňĻevenňļ the Messenger and the believers with him cried out, ‚ÄúWhen will Allah‚Äôs help come?‚ÄĚ Indeed, Allah‚Äôs help is ňĻalwaysňļ near.

2:215.text: They ask you ňĻO Prophet inňļ what ňĻwayňļ they should donate. Say, ‚ÄúWhatever donations you give are for parents, relatives, orphans, the poor, and ňĻneedyňļ travellers. Whatever good you do is certainly well known to Allah.‚ÄĚ

2:216.text: Fighting has been made obligatory upon you ňĻbelieversňļ, though you dislike it. Perhaps you dislike something which is good for you and like something which is bad for you. Allah knows and you do not know.

2:217.text: They<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†ask you ňĻO Prophetňļ about fighting in the sacred months.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†Say, ‚ÄúFighting during these months is a great sin. But hindering ňĻothersňļ from the Path of Allah, rejecting Him, and expelling the worshippers from the Sacred Mosque is ňĻaňļ greater ňĻsinňļ in the sight of Allah. For persecution<sup foot_note=3>3</sup>¬†is far worse than killing. And they will not stop fighting you until they turn you away from your faith‚ÄĒif they can. And whoever among you renounces their own faith and dies a disbeliever, their deeds will become void in this life and in the Hereafter. It is they who will be the residents of the Fire. They will be there forever.‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=4>4</sup>
footnotes: ¬†The pagans of Mecca.
¬†See¬†footnote for 2:194.
¬†Persecuting Muslims to abandon their faith.

¬†While many traditional scholars maintain that apostates are to be first invited to re-enter the fold of Islam then executed if the invitation is turned down, some traditional and modern scholars (like Sufy√Ęn Ath-Thawri, the late Grand Im√Ęm of Al-Azhar Sheikh MaŠł•m√Ľd Shalt√Ľt, and Dr. Jamal Badawi) are of the opinion that the Quran (2:256) guarantees freedom of religion and (2:217) promises no worldly punishment for leaving Islam. It is reported in a Šł•ad√ģth¬†collected by Im√Ęm Muslim that a man came to the Prophet (Ô∑ļ) in Medina and retracted the allegiance he had pledged to him (Ô∑ļ)‚ÄĒleaving Islam. The Prophet (Ô∑ļ) did not punish the man. The narrations that command the killing of apostates refer only to those who fight against Muslims‚ÄĒso they are killed for treason, not for apostasy. Other aŠł•√Ęd√ģth¬†are meant to deter those who conspired to accept Islam then leave it soon after only to shake the faith of early Muslims, as mentioned in 3:72.

2:218.text: Surely those who have believed, emigrated, and struggled in the Way of Allah‚ÄĒthey can hope for Allah‚Äôs mercy. And Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.

2:219.text: They ask you ňĻO Prophetňļ about intoxicants and gambling. Say, ‚ÄúThere is great evil in both, as well as some benefit for people‚ÄĒbut the evil outweighs the benefit.‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†They ňĻalsoňļ ask you ňĻO Prophetňļ what they should donate. Say, ‚ÄúWhatever you can spare.‚ÄĚ This is how Allah makes His revelations clear to you ňĻbelieversňļ, so perhaps you may reflect
footnotes: ¬†See¬†footnote for 4:43.

2:220.text: upon this world and the Hereafter. And they ask you ňĻO Prophetňļ concerning orphans. Say, ‚ÄúImproving their condition is best. And if you partner with them, they are bonded with you ňĻin faithňļ.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†And Allah knows who intends harm and who intends good. Had Allah willed, He could have made it difficult for you.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†Surely Allah is Almighty, All-Wise.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†Literal translation, ‚ÄúThey are your brothers.‚ÄĚ
¬†By not allowing partnership between you and orphans.

2:221.text: Do not marry polytheistic women until they believe; for a believing slave-woman is better than a free polytheist, even though she may look pleasant to you. And do not marry your women to polytheistic men until they believe, for a believing slave-man is better than a free polytheist, even though he may look pleasant to you. They invite ňĻyouňļ to the Fire while Allah invites ňĻyouňļ to Paradise and forgiveness by His grace.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†He makes His revelations clear to the people so perhaps they will be mindful.
footnotes: ¬†lit., ‚Äúby His permission.‚ÄĚ

2:222.text: They ask you ňĻO Prophetňļ about menstruation. Say, ‚ÄúBeware of its harm! So keep away, and do not have intercourse with your wives during their monthly cycles until they are purified.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†When they purify themselves, then you may approach them in the manner specified by Allah. Surely Allah loves those who always turn to Him in repentance and those who purify themselves.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†Do not have intercourse with them until their menstruation is over and they have taken a full bath.

2:223.text: Your wives are like farmland for you,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†so approach them ňĻconsensuallyňļ as you please.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†And send forth something good for yourselves.<sup foot_note=3>3</sup>¬†Be mindful of Allah, and know that you will meet Him. And give good news to the believers.
footnotes: ¬†Šł§arth means ‚Äúfarmland‚ÄĚ‚ÄĒthe husband being like a farmer, the wife like productive land, and children like seeds‚ÄĒa metaphor for fertility and growth.
¬†It is permissible to have intimate relations in any position. However vaginal penetration during monthly cycles or while bleeding persists after childbirth (up to forty days) is not permitted.

¬†This can either imply ‚Äúsending forth good deeds that will be rewarded in the next life‚ÄĚ or‚ÄĒaccording to some interpretations‚ÄĒ‚Äúengaging in foreplay with one‚Äôs spouse before intercourse.‚ÄĚ

2:224.text: Do not use Allah‚Äôs Name in your oaths as an excuse for not doing good, not guarding against evil, or not making peace between people. And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

2:225.text: Allah will not hold you accountable for unintentional oaths, but for what you intended in your hearts. And Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Forbearing.

2:226.text: Those who swear not to have intercourse with their wives must wait for four months.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†If they change their mind, then Allah is certainly All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.
footnotes: ¬†This ruling is called il√Ę'. Il√Ę' means that a husband vows not to be intimate with his wife because of a dispute or any other legitimate reason. The verse here says if it is four months or less and the husband keeps his vow, then he does not have to make up for breaking his vow (by feeding ten poor people, or fasting three days), otherwise he will need to make up for breaking his vow. If the vow is made for over four months, the wife has the right to seek divorce after four months. Il√Ę' should be avoided altogether since it deprives the wife of her right to sexual satisfaction. Instead, counselling is a better alternative at the time of dispute.

2:227.text: But if they settle on divorce, then Allah is indeed All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

2:228.text: Divorced women must wait three monthly cycles ňĻbefore they can re-marryňļ. It is not lawful for them to conceal what Allah has created in their wombs,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†if they ňĻtrulyňļ believe in Allah and the Last Day. And their husbands reserve the right to take them back within that period if they desire reconciliation. Women have rights similar to those of men equitably, although men have a degree ňĻof responsibilityňļ above them. And Allah is Almighty, All-Wise.
footnotes: ¬†This could mean ‚Äúpregnancy‚ÄĚ or ‚Äúaccurate information about monthly cycles.‚ÄĚ

2:229.text: Divorce may be retracted twice, then the husband must retain ňĻhis wifeňļ with honour or separate ňĻfrom herňļ with grace.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†It is not lawful for husbands to take back anything of the dowry given to their wives, unless the couple fears not being able to keep within the limits of Allah.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†So if you fear they will not be able to keep within the limits of Allah, there is no blame if the wife compensates the husband to obtain divorce.<sup foot_note=3>3</sup>¬†These are the limits set by Allah, so do not transgress them. And whoever transgresses the limits of Allah, they are the ňĻtrueňļ wrongdoers.
footnotes: ¬†A husband may separate from his wife after each of the first two counts of divorce or at the end of her waiting period (see¬†65:1-5) with dignity. If he chooses to stay with her after the first two counts of divorce then divorces her a third time, the marriage is terminated at the end of her third waiting period. The wife will have to marry and divorce another man before she can be remarried to her ex-husband (see¬†2:230). However, a woman marrying someone with the intention of getting divorced, in order to return to her first husband, is forbidden.
¬†The ‚Äúlimits of Allah‚ÄĚ implies fidelity to one‚Äôs spouse according to Allah‚Äôs commands.
¬†If the wife does not want to continue in the marriage for legitimate reasons, then she can return the dowry (mahr) to the husband in compensation for divorce. This ruling is called khul‚Äô.

2:230.text: So if a husband divorces his wife ňĻthree timesňļ, then it is not lawful for him to remarry her until after she has married another man and then is divorced. Then it is permissible for them to reunite, as long as they feel they are able to maintain the limits of Allah. These are the limits set by Allah, which He makes clear for people of knowledge.

2:231.text: When you divorce women and they have ňĻalmostňļ reached the end of their waiting period, either retain them honourably or let them go honourably. But do not retain them ňĻonlyňļ to harm them ňĻorňļ to take advantage ňĻof themňļ. Whoever does that surely wrongs his own soul. Do not take Allah‚Äôs revelations lightly. Remember Allah‚Äôs favours upon you as well as the Book and wisdom<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†He has sent down for your guidance. Be mindful of Allah, and know that Allah has ňĻperfectňļ knowledge of all things.
footnotes: ¬†‚ÄúWisdom‚ÄĚ means the ‚Äúsunnah‚ÄĚ or the tradition of the Prophet (Ô∑ļ) when it is mentioned along with the Book (i.e., the Quran).

2:232.text: When you divorce women and they have reached the end of their waiting period, do not ňĻlet the guardiansňļ prevent them from re-marrying their ex-husbands if they come to an honourable agreement. This is enjoined on whoever has faith in Allah and the Last Day. This is purer and more dignifying for you. Allah knows and you do not know.

2:233.text: ňĻDivorcedňļ mothers will breastfeed their offspring for two whole years, for those who wish to complete the nursing ňĻof their childňļ. The child‚Äôs father will provide reasonable maintenance and clothing for the mother ňĻduring that periodňļ. No one will be charged with more than they can bear. No mother or father should be made to suffer for their child. The ňĻfather‚Äôsňļ heirs are under the same obligation. But if both sides decide‚ÄĒafter mutual consultation and consent‚ÄĒto wean a child, then there is no blame on them. If you decide to have your children nursed by a wet-nurse, it is permissible as long as you pay fairly. Be mindful of Allah, and know that Allah is All-Seeing of what you do.

2:234.text: As for those of you who die and leave widows behind, let them observe a waiting period of four months and ten days. When they have reached the end of this period, then you<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†are not accountable for what they decide for themselves in a reasonable manner. And Allah is All-Aware of what you do.
footnotes: ¬†i.e., the guardians.

2:235.text: There is no blame on you for subtly showing interest in ňĻdivorced or widowedňļ women<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†or for hiding ňĻthe intentionňļ in your hearts. Allah knows that you are considering them ňĻfor marriageňļ. But do not make a secret commitment with them‚ÄĒyou can only show interest in them appropriately. Do not commit to the bond of marriage until the waiting period expires. Know that Allah is aware of what is in your hearts, so beware of Him. And know that Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Forbearing.
footnotes: ¬†During their waiting periods.

2:236.text: There is no blame if you divorce women before the marriage is consummated or the dowry is settled. But give them a ňĻsuitableňļ compensation‚ÄĒthe rich according to his means and the poor according to his. A reasonable compensation is an obligation on the good-doers.

2:237.text: And if you divorce them before consummating the marriage but after deciding on a dowry, pay half of the dowry, unless the wife graciously waives it or the husband graciously pays in full. Graciousness is closer to righteousness. And do not forget kindness among yourselves. Surely Allah is All-Seeing of what you do.

2:238.text: Observe the ňĻfive obligatoryňļ prayers‚ÄĒespecially the middle prayer<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>‚ÄĒand stand in true devotion to Allah.
footnotes: ¬†As mentioned in several authentic sayings of Prophet MuŠł•ammad (Ô∑ļ), the middle prayer is the afternoon prayer, ‚ÄôAŠĻ£r.

2:239.text: If you are in danger, pray on foot or while riding. But when you are safe, ňĻtake time toňļ remember Allah for teaching you what you did not know.

2:240.text: Those of you who die leaving widows should bequeath for them a year‚Äôs maintenance without forcing them out.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†But if they choose to leave, you are not accountable for what they reasonably decide for themselves. And Allah is Almighty, All-Wise.
footnotes: ¬†This ruling was later replaced by the ruling in 2:234.

2:241.text: Reasonable provisions must be made for divorced women‚ÄĒa duty on those mindful ňĻof Allahňļ.

2:242.text: This is how Allah makes His revelations clear to you, so perhaps you will understand.

2:243.text: Have you ňĻO Prophetňļ not seen those who fled their homes in the thousands for fear of death?<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Allah said to them, ‚ÄúDie!‚ÄĚ then He gave them life. Surely Allah is ever Bountiful to humanity, but most people are ungrateful.
footnotes: ¬†They fled in the wake of a plague or an enemy attack.

2:244.text: Fight in the cause of Allah, and know that Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

2:245.text: Who will lend to Allah a good loan which Allah will multiply many times over? It is Allah ňĻaloneňļ who decreases and increases ňĻwealthňļ. And to Him you will ňĻallňļ be returned.

2:246.text: Have you not seen those chiefs of the Children of Israel after Moses? They said to one of their prophets, ‚ÄúAppoint for us a king, ňĻandňļ we will fight in the cause of Allah.‚ÄĚ He said, ‚ÄúAre you not going to cower if ordered to fight?‚ÄĚ They replied, ‚ÄúHow could we refuse to fight in the cause of Allah, while we were driven out of our homes and ňĻseparated fromňļ our children?‚ÄĚ But when they were ordered to fight, they fled, except for a few of them. And Allah has ňĻperfectňļ knowledge of the wrongdoers.

2:247.text: Their prophet told them, ‚ÄúAllah has appointed Saul<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†to be your king.‚ÄĚ They protested, ‚ÄúHow can he be our king when some of us are more deserving of kingship than he, and he has not been blessed with vast riches?‚ÄĚ He replied, ‚ÄúAllah has chosen him over you and blessed him with knowledge and stature. Allah grants kingship to whoever He wills. And Allah is All-Bountiful, All-Knowing.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†ŠĻ¨al√ĽŠĻ≠.

2:248.text: Their prophet further told them, ‚ÄúThe sign of Saul‚Äôs kingship is that the Ark will come to you‚ÄĒcontaining reassurance<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†from your Lord and relics of the family of Moses and the family of Aaron,<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†which will be carried by the angels. Surely in this is a sign for you, if you ňĻtrulyňļ believe.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†Reassurance refers to the Torah.
¬†i.e., the staff of Moses and fragments of the Tablets.

2:249.text: When Saul marched forth with his army, he cautioned: ‚ÄúAllah will test you with a river. So whoever drinks ňĻhis fillňļ from it is not with me, and whoever does not taste it‚ÄĒexcept a sip from the hollow of his hands‚ÄĒis definitely with me.‚ÄĚ They all drank ňĻtheir fillňļ except for a few! When he and the ňĻremainingňļ faithful with him crossed the river, they said, ‚ÄúNow we are no match for Goliath and his warriors.‚ÄĚ But those ňĻbelieversňļ who were certain they would meet Allah reasoned, ‚ÄúHow many times has a small force vanquished a mighty army by the Will of Allah! And Allah is ňĻalwaysňļ with the steadfast.‚ÄĚ

2:250.text: When they advanced to face Goliath and his warriors, they prayed, ‚ÄúOur Lord! Shower us with perseverance, make our steps firm, and give us victory over the disbelieving people.‚ÄĚ

2:251.text: So they defeated them by Allah‚Äôs Will, and David killed Goliath. And Allah blessed David with kingship and wisdom and taught him what He willed. Had Allah not repelled a group of people by ňĻthe might ofňļ another, corruption would have dominated the earth, but Allah is Gracious to all.

2:252.text: These are Allah‚Äôs revelations which We recite to you ňĻO Prophetňļ in truth. And you are truly one of the messengers.

2:253.text: We have chosen some of those messengers above others.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Allah spoke directly to some, and raised some high in rank. To Jesus, son of Mary, We gave clear proofs and supported him with the holy spirit.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†If Allah had willed, succeeding generations would not have fought ňĻamong themselvesňļ after receiving the clear proofs. But they differed‚ÄĒsome believed while others disbelieved. Yet if Allah had willed, they would not have fought one another. But Allah does what He wills.
footnotes: ¬†Those messengers mentioned earlier in this s√Ľrah¬†(see¬†verse 136).
¬†i.e., Gabriel.

2:254.text: O believers! Donate from what We have provided for you before the arrival of a Day when there will be no bargaining,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†friendship,<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†or intercession. Those who disbelieve are ňĻtrulyňļ the wrongdoers.
footnotes: ¬†This implies ransoming one‚Äôs self.
¬†i.e., friendly connections.

2:255.text: Allah! There is no god ňĻworthy of worshipňļ except Him, the Ever-Living, All-Sustaining. Neither drowsiness nor sleep overtakes Him. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who could possibly intercede with Him without His permission? He ňĻfullyňļ knows what is ahead of them and what is behind them, but no one can grasp any of His knowledge‚ÄĒexcept what He wills ňĻto revealňļ. His Seat<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†encompasses the heavens and the earth, and the preservation of both does not tire Him. For He is the Most High, the Greatest.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†
footnotes: ¬†The Arabic word kursi¬†can either mean seat or knowledge. There are some narrations attributed to Prophet MuŠł•ammad (Ô∑ļ) that describe Allah‚Äôs Throne (‚ÄôArsh) as being greater than His Kursi.
¬†According to Muslim belief, this is the greatest verse in the Quran.

2:256.text: Let there be no compulsion in religion, for the truth stands out clearly from falsehood.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†So whoever renounces false gods and believes in Allah has certainly grasped the firmest, unfailing hand-hold. And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.
footnotes: ¬†This verse was revealed when some new Muslims tried to force their Jewish and Christian children to convert to Islam after the Prophet‚Äôs emigration (Hijrah) to Medina. The verse prohibits forced conversion.

2:257.text: Allah is the Guardian of the believers‚ÄĒHe brings them out of darkness and into light. As for the disbelievers, their guardians are false gods who lead them out of light and into darkness. It is they who will be the residents of the Fire. They will be there forever.

2:258.text: Are you ňĻO Prophetňļ not aware of the one who argued with Abraham about his Lord because Allah had granted him kingship? ňĻRememberňļ when Abraham said, ‚ÄúMy Lord is the One Who has power to give life and cause death.‚ÄĚ He argued, ‚ÄúI too have the power to give life and cause death.‚ÄĚ Abraham challenged ňĻhimňļ, ‚ÄúAllah causes the sun to rise from the east. So make it rise from the west.‚ÄĚ And so the disbeliever was dumbstruck. And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people.

2:259.text: Or ňĻare you not aware ofňļ the one who passed by a city which was in ruins. He wondered, ‚ÄúHow could Allah bring this back to life after its destruction?‚ÄĚ So Allah caused him to die for a hundred years then brought him back to life. Allah asked, ‚ÄúHow long have you remained ňĻin this stateňļ?‚ÄĚ He replied, ‚ÄúPerhaps a day or part of a day.‚ÄĚ Allah said, ‚ÄúNo! You have remained here for a hundred years! Just look at your food and drink‚ÄĒthey have not spoiled. ňĻBut nowňļ look at ňĻthe remains ofňļ your donkey! And ňĻsoňļ We have made you into a sign for humanity. And look at the bones ňĻof the donkeyňļ, how We bring them together then clothe them with flesh!‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†When this was made clear to him, he declared, ‚ÄúňĻNowňļ I know that Allah is Most Capable of everything.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†Bringing the donkey back to life.

2:260.text: And ňĻrememberňļ when Abraham said, ‚ÄúMy Lord! Show me how you give life to the dead.‚ÄĚ Allah responded, ‚ÄúDo you not believe?‚ÄĚ Abraham replied, ‚ÄúYes I do, but just so my heart can be reassured.‚ÄĚ Allah said, ‚ÄúThen bring four birds, train them to come to you, ňĻthen cut them into pieces,ňļ and scatter them on different hilltops. Then call them back, they will fly to you in haste. And ňĻso you willňļ know that Allah is Almighty, All-Wise.‚ÄĚ

2:261.text: The example of those who spend their wealth in the cause of Allah is that of a grain that sprouts into seven ears, each bearing one hundred grains. And Allah multiplies ňĻthe reward even moreňļ to whoever He wills. For Allah is All-Bountiful, All-Knowing.

2:262.text: Those who spend their wealth in the cause of Allah and do not follow their charity with reminders of their generosity or hurtful words‚ÄĒthey will get their reward from their Lord, and there will be no fear for them, nor will they grieve.

2:263.text: Kind words and forgiveness are better than charity followed by injury. And Allah is Self-Sufficient, Most Forbearing.

2:264.text: O believers! Do not waste your charity with reminders ňĻof your generosityňļ or hurtful words, like those who donate their wealth just to show off and do not believe in Allah or the Last Day. Their example is that of a hard barren rock covered with a thin layer of soil hit by a strong rain‚ÄĒleaving it just a bare stone. Such people are unable to preserve the reward of their charity. Allah does not guide ňĻsuchňļ disbelieving people.

2:265.text: And the example of those who donate their wealth, seeking Allah‚Äôs pleasure and believing the reward is certain,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†is that of a garden on a fertile hill: when heavy rain falls, it yields up twice its normal produce. If no heavy rain falls, a drizzle is sufficient. And Allah is All-Seeing of what you do.
footnotes: ¬†It could also mean ‚Äú‚Ä¶ seeking Allah‚Äôs pleasure and proving their sincerity.‚ÄĚ

2:266.text: Would any of you wish to have a garden with palm trees, grapevines, and all kinds of fruits with rivers flowing underneath and as they grow very old with dependent children, a fiery whirlwind hits the garden, burning it all up? This is how Allah makes His revelations clear to you, so perhaps you will reflect.

2:267.text: O believers! Donate from the best of what you have earned and of what We have produced for you from the earth. Do not pick out worthless things for donation, which you yourselves would only accept with closed eyes. And know that Allah is Self-Sufficient, Praiseworthy.

2:268.text: The Devil threatens you with ňĻthe prospect ofňļ poverty and bids you to the shameful deed ňĻof stinginessňļ, while Allah promises you forgiveness and ňĻgreatňļ bounties from Him. And Allah is All-Bountiful, All-Knowing.

2:269.text: He grants wisdom to whoever He wills. And whoever is granted wisdom is certainly blessed with a great privilege. But none will be mindful ňĻof thisňļ except people of reason.

2:270.text: Whatever charities you give or vows you make are surely known to Allah. And the wrongdoers will have no helpers.

2:271.text: To give charity publicly is good, but to give to the poor privately is better for you, and will absolve you of your sins. And Allah is All-Aware of what you do.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Donations for a public cause may be done openly to encourage others to donate, whereas it is recommended to give charity to the poor secretly.

2:272.text: You are not responsible for people‚Äôs guidance ňĻO Prophetňļ‚ÄĒit is Allah Who guides whoever He wills. Whatever you ňĻbelieversňļ spend in charity, it is for your own good‚ÄĒas long as you do so seeking the pleasure of Allah.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Whatever you donate will be paid back to you in full, and you will not be wronged.
footnotes: ¬†lit., seeking the Face of Allah.

2:273.text: ňĻCharity isňļ for the needy who are too engaged in the cause of Allah to move about in the land ňĻfor workňļ. Those unfamiliar with their situation will think they are not in need ňĻof charityňļ because they do not beg. You can recognize them by their appearance. They do not beg people persistently. Whatever you give in charity is certainly well known to Allah.

2:274.text: Those who spend their wealth in charity day and night, secretly and openly‚ÄĒtheir reward is with their Lord, and there will be no fear for them, nor will they grieve.

2:275.text: Those who consume interest will stand ňĻon Judgment Dayňļ like those driven to madness by Satan‚Äôs touch. That is because they say, ‚ÄúTrade is no different than interest.‚ÄĚ But Allah has permitted trading and forbidden interest. Whoever refrains‚ÄĒafter having received warning from their Lord‚ÄĒmay keep their previous gains, and their case is left to Allah. As for those who persist, it is they who will be the residents of the Fire. They will be there forever.

2:276.text: Allah has made interest fruitless and charity fruitful. And Allah does not like any ungrateful evildoer.

2:277.text: Indeed, those who believe, do good, establish prayer, and pay alms-tax will receive their reward from their Lord, and there will be no fear for them, nor will they grieve.

2:278.text: O believers! Fear Allah, and give up outstanding interest if you are ňĻtrueňļ believers.

2:279.text: If you do not, then beware of a war with Allah and His Messenger! But if you repent, you may retain your principal‚ÄĒneither inflicting nor suffering harm.

2:280.text: If it is difficult for someone to repay a debt, postpone it until a time of ease. And if you waive it as an act of charity, it will be better for you, if only you knew.

2:281.text: Be mindful of the Day when you will ňĻallňļ be returned to Allah, then every soul will be paid in full for what it has done, and none will be wronged.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Verse 281 is believed by many scholars to be the last verse revealed of the Quran.

2:282.text: O believers! When you contract a loan for a fixed period of time, commit it to writing. Let the scribe maintain justice between the parties. The scribe should not refuse to write as Allah has taught them to write. They will write what the debtor dictates, bearing Allah in mind and not defrauding the debt. If the debtor is incompetent, weak, or unable to dictate, let their guardian dictate for them with justice. Call upon two of your men to witness. If two men cannot be found, then one man and two women of your choice will witness‚ÄĒso if one of the women forgets the other may remind her.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†The witnesses must not refuse when they are summoned. You must not be against writing ňĻcontractsňļ for a fixed period‚ÄĒwhether the sum is small or great. This is more just ňĻfor youňļ in the sight of Allah, and more convenient to establish evidence and remove doubts. However, if you conduct an immediate transaction among yourselves, then there is no need for you to record it, but call upon witnesses when a deal is finalized. Let no harm come to the scribe or witnesses. If you do, then you have gravely exceeded ňĻyour limitsňļ. Be mindful of Allah, for Allah ňĻis the One Whoňļ teaches you. And Allah has ňĻperfectňļ knowledge of all things.
footnotes: ¬†Generally speaking, there is a difference between witnessing and giving testimony before a judge. Verse 2:282 talks about witnessing a debt contract, not giving testimony. To fully understand the context of this verse, we need to keep in mind that 1500 years ago women did not normally participate in business transactions or travel with trading caravans and, therefore, not every woman had the expertise to witness a debt contract. Even if two women were available at the time of signing the contract, perhaps the primary witness might not be able to recall the details of the contract or appear before a judge because of compelling circumstances such as pregnancy or delivery. In any of these cases, the second woman will be a back-up. Some scholars maintain that one woman can be sufficient as a witness so long as she is reliable. As for giving testimony, a ruling can be made based on available testimony, regardless of the number or gender of the witnesses. For example, the beginning of RamaŠłć√Ęn is usually confirmed by the sighting of the new moon, regardless of the gender of the person who sights the moon. Also the highest form of witness in Islam is for someone to testify they heard a narration (or Šł•ad√ģth) from the Prophet (Ô∑ļ). An authentic Šł•ad√ģth¬†is accepted by all Muslims regardless of the gender of the narrator. Moreover, if a husband accuses his wife of adultery and he has no witnesses, each spouse must testify five times that they are telling the truth and the other side is lying. Both testimonies are equal (see¬†24:6-10). In some cases, only women‚Äôs testimony is accepted while men‚Äôs testimony is rejected, such as testifying regarding a woman‚Äôs pregnancy or virginity.

2:283.text: If you are on a journey and a scribe cannot be found, then a security can be taken. If you trust one another, then ňĻthere is no need for a security, butňļ the debtor should honour this trust ňĻby repaying the debtňļ‚ÄĒand let them fear Allah, their Lord. And do not conceal the testimony, for whoever conceals it, their hearts are indeed sinful. And Allah ňĻfullyňļ knows what you do.

2:284.text: To Allah ňĻaloneňļ belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Whether you reveal what is in your hearts or conceal it, Allah will call you to account for it. He forgives whoever He wills, and punishes whoever He wills. And Allah is Most Capable of everything.

2:285.text: The Messenger ňĻfirmlyňļ believes in what has been revealed to him from his Lord, and so do the believers. They ňĻallňļ believe in Allah, His angels, His Books, and His messengers. ňĻThey proclaim,ňļ ‚ÄúWe make no distinction between any of His messengers.‚ÄĚ And they say, ‚ÄúWe hear and obey. ňĻWe seekňļ Your forgiveness, our Lord! And to You ňĻaloneňļ is the final return.‚ÄĚ

2:286.text: Allah does not require of any soul more than what it can afford. All good will be for its own benefit, and all evil will be to its own loss. ňĻThe believers pray,ňļ ‚ÄúOur Lord! Do not punish us if we forget or make a mistake. Our Lord! Do not place a burden on us like the one you placed on those before us. Our Lord! Do not burden us with what we cannot bear. Pardon us, forgive us, and have mercy on us. You are our ňĻonlyňļ Guardian. So grant us victory over the disbelieving people.‚ÄĚ

20:2.text: We have not revealed the Quran to you ňĻO Prophetňļ to cause you distress,

20:3.text: but as a reminder to those in awe ňĻof Allahňļ.

20:4.text: ňĻIt isňļ a revelation from the One Who created the earth and the high heavens‚ÄĒ

20:5.text: the Most Compassionate, ňĻWho isňļ established on the Throne.

20:6.text: To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth and whatever is in between and whatever is underground.

20:7.text: Whether you speak openly ňĻor notňļ, He certainly knows what is secret and what is even more hidden.

20:8.text: Allah‚ÄĒthere is no god ňĻworthy of worshipňļ except Him. He has the Most Beautiful Names.

20:9.text: Has the story of Moses reached you ňĻO Prophetňļ?

20:10.text: When he saw a fire, he said to his family, ‚ÄúWait here, ňĻforňļ I have spotted¬†a fire. Perhaps I can bring you a torch from it, or find some guidance at the fire.‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Moses and his family lost their way in the dark while they were travelling from Midian to Egypt.

20:11.text: But when he approached it, he was called, ‚ÄúO Moses!

20:12.text: It is truly I. I am your Lord! So take off your sandals, for you are in the sacred valley of ŠĻ¨uwa.

20:13.text: I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed:

20:14.text: ‚ÄėIt is truly I. I am Allah! There is no god ňĻworthy of worshipňļ except Me. So worship Me ňĻaloneňļ, and establish prayer for My remembrance.

20:15.text: The Hour is sure to come. My Will is to keep it hidden, so that every soul may be rewarded according to their efforts.

20:16.text: So do not let those who disbelieve in it and follow their desires distract you from it, or you will be doomed.‚Äô‚ÄĚ

20:17.text: ňĻAllah added,ňļ ‚ÄúAnd what is that in your right hand, O ¬†Moses?‚ÄĚ

20:18.text: He replied, ‚ÄúIt is my staff! I lean on it, and with it I beat down ňĻbranchesňļ for my sheep, and have other uses for it.‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Moses could have just said, ‚ÄúA staff.‚ÄĚ But he volunteered to talk about the uses of the staff, adding that there are many other uses he has not mentioned, with the hope that Allah would ask him about it more. The reason is that Moses wanted to prolong the conversation with Allah as much as he could.

20:20.text: So he did, then‚ÄĒbehold!‚ÄĒit became a serpent, slithering.

20:21.text: Allah said, ‚ÄúTake it, and have no fear. We will return it to its former state.

20:22.text: And put your hand under your armpit, it will come out ňĻshiningňļ white, unblemished,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†as another sign,
footnotes: ¬†Moses, who was dark-skinned, was asked to put his hand under his armpit. When he took it out it was shining white, but not out of a skin condition like vitiligo.

20:23.text: so that We may show you some of Our greatest signs.

20:24.text: Go to Pharaoh, for he has truly transgressed ňĻall boundsňļ.‚ÄĚ

20:25.text: Moses prayed, ‚ÄúMy Lord! Uplift my heart for me,

20:27.text: and remove the impediment from my tongue

20:28.text: so people may understand my speech,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Moses (Ô∑ļ) put a brand of fire in his mouth, which hindered his speech as he grew up. In this verse, he prays to Allah to help him speak clearly, and his prayer is answered.

20:29.text: and grant me a helper from my family,

20:32.text: and let him share my task,

20:33.text: so that we may glorify You much

20:35.text: for truly You have ňĻalwaysňļ been overseeing us.‚ÄĚ

20:36.text: Allah responded, ‚ÄúAll that you requested has been granted, O ¬†Moses!

20:37.text: And surely We had shown you favour before,

20:38.text: when We inspired your mother with this:

20:39.text: ‚ÄėPut him into a chest, then put it into the river. The river will wash it ashore, and he will be taken by ňĻPharaoh,ňļ an enemy of Mine and his.‚Äô And I endeared you with love from Me ňĻO Mosesňļ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†so that you would be brought up under My ňĻwatchfulňļ Eye.
footnotes: ¬†In other words, Allah made Moses a likeable person so Pharaoh and his wife would agree to keep him.

20:40.text: ňĻRememberňļ when your sister came along and proposed, ‚ÄėShall I direct you to someone who will nurse him?‚Äô<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†So We reunited you with your mother so that her heart would be put at ease, and she would not grieve. ňĻLaterňļ you killed a man ňĻby mistakeňļ, but We saved you from sorrow, as well as other tests We put you through. Then you stayed for a number of years among the people of Midian. Then you came here as pre-destined, O ¬†Moses!
footnotes: ¬†Moses had refused all wet-nurses that were brought for him.

20:41.text: And I have selected you for My service.

20:42.text: Go forth, you and your brother, with My signs and never falter in remembering Me.

20:43.text: Go, both of you, to Pharaoh, for he has truly transgressed ňĻall boundsňļ.

20:44.text: Speak to him gently, so perhaps he may be mindful ňĻof Meňļ or fearful ňĻof My punishmentňļ.‚ÄĚ

20:45.text: They both pleaded, ‚ÄúOur Lord! We fear that he may be quick to harm us or act tyrannically.‚ÄĚ

20:46.text: Allah reassured ňĻthemňļ, ‚ÄúHave no fear! I am with you, hearing and seeing.

20:47.text: So go to him and say, ‚ÄėIndeed we are both messengers from your Lord, so let the Children of Israel go with us, and do not oppress them. We have come to you with a sign from your Lord. And salvation will be for whoever follows the ňĻrightňļ guidance.

20:48.text: It has indeed been revealed to us that the punishment will be upon whoever denies ňĻthe truthňļ and turns away.‚Äô‚ÄĚ

20:49.text: Pharaoh asked, ‚ÄúWho then is the Lord of you two, O ¬†Moses?‚ÄĚ

20:50.text: He answered, ‚ÄúOur Lord is the One Who has given everything its ňĻdistinctiveňļ form, then guided ňĻitňļ.‚ÄĚ

20:51.text: Pharaoh asked, ‚ÄúAnd what about previous peoples?‚ÄĚ

20:52.text: He replied, ‚ÄúThat knowledge is with my Lord in a Record. My Lord neither falters¬†nor forgets ňĻanythingňļ.‚ÄĚ

20:53.text: ňĻHe is the Oneňļ Who has laid out the earth for ňĻall ofňļ you, and set in it pathways for you, and sends down rain from the sky, causing various types of plants to grow,

20:54.text: ňĻsoňļ eat and graze your cattle. Surely in this are signs for people of sound judgment.

20:55.text: From the earth We created you, and into it We will return you, and from it We will bring you back again.

20:56.text: And We certainly showed Pharaoh all of Our signs,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†but he denied them and refused ňĻto believeňļ.
footnotes: ¬†See¬†footnote for 17:101.

20:57.text: He said, ‚ÄúHave you come to drive us out of our land with your magic, O ¬†Moses?

20:58.text: We can surely meet you with similar magic. So set for us an appointment that neither of us will fail to keep, in a central place.‚ÄĚ

20:59.text: Moses said, ‚ÄúYour appointment is on the Day of the Festival, and let the people be gathered mid-morning.‚ÄĚ

20:60.text: Pharaoh then withdrew, orchestrated his scheme, then returned.

20:61.text: Moses warned the magicians, ‚ÄúWoe to you! Do not fabricate a lie against Allah,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†or He will wipe you out with a torment. Whoever fabricates ňĻliesňļ is bound to fail.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†By saying that Moses‚Äô miracle is pure magic.

20:62.text: So the magicians disputed the matter among themselves, conversing privately.

20:63.text: They concluded, ‚ÄúThese two are only magicians who want to drive you out of your land with their magic, and do away with your most cherished traditions.

20:64.text: So orchestrate your plan, then come forward in ňĻperfectňļ ranks. And whoever prevails today will certainly be successful.‚ÄĚ

20:65.text: They said, ‚ÄúO Moses! Either you cast, or let us be the first to cast.‚ÄĚ

20:66.text: Moses responded, ‚ÄúNo, you go first.‚ÄĚ And suddenly their ropes and staffs appeared to him‚ÄĒby their magic‚ÄĒto be slithering.

20:67.text: So Moses concealed fear within himself.

20:68.text: We reassured ňĻhimňļ, ‚ÄúDo not fear! It is certainly you who will prevail.

20:69.text: Cast what is in your right hand, and it will swallow up what they have made, for what they have made is no more than a magic trick. And magicians can never succeed wherever they go.‚ÄĚ

20:70.text: So the magicians fell down in prostration, declaring, ‚ÄúWe believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses.‚ÄĚ

20:71.text: Pharaoh threatened, ‚ÄúHow dare you believe in him before I give you permission? He must be your master who taught you magic. I will certainly cut off your hands and feet on opposite sides, and crucify you on the trunks of palm trees. You will really see whose punishment is more severe and more lasting.‚ÄĚ

20:72.text: They responded, ‚ÄúBy the One Who created us! We will never prefer you over the clear proofs that have come to us. So do whatever you want! Your authority only covers the ňĻfleetingňļ life of this world.

20:73.text: Indeed, we have believed in our Lord so He may forgive our sins and that magic you have forced us to practice. And Allah is far superior ňĻin rewardňļ and more lasting ňĻin punishmentňļ.‚ÄĚ

20:74.text: Whoever comes to their Lord as an evildoer will certainly have Hell, where they can neither live nor die.

20:75.text: But whoever comes to Him as a believer, having done good, they will have the highest ranks:

20:76.text: the Gardens of Eternity, under which rivers flow, where they will stay forever. That is the reward of those who purify themselves.

20:77.text: And We surely inspired Moses, ňĻsaying,ňļ ‚ÄúLeave with My servants ňĻat nightňļ and strike a dry passage for them across the sea. Have no fear of being overtaken, nor be concerned ňĻof drowningňļ.‚ÄĚ

20:78.text: Then Pharaoh pursued them with his soldiers‚ÄĒbut how overwhelming were the waters that submerged them!

20:79.text: And ňĻsoňļ Pharaoh led his people astray, and did not guide ňĻthem rightlyňļ.

20:80.text: O Children of Israel! We saved you from your enemy, and made an appointment with you<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†on the right side of Mount ŠĻ¨√Ľr, and sent down to you manna and quails,<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>
footnotes: ¬†i.e., your prophet to receive the Tablets for your guidance.
¬†Manna (heavenly bread) and quails (chicken-like birds) sustained the children of Israel in the wilderness after they left Egypt.

20:81.text: ňĻsaying,ňļ ‚ÄúEat from the good things We have provided for you, but do not transgress in them, or My wrath will befall you. And whoever My wrath befalls is certainly doomed.

20:82.text: But I am truly Most Forgiving to whoever repents, believes, and does good, then persists on ňĻtrueňļ guidance.‚ÄĚ

20:83.text: ňĻAllah asked,ňļ ‚ÄúWhy have you come with such haste ahead of your people, O ¬†Moses?‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Moses selected a delegation of seventy people from his community to go to Mount ŠĻ¨√Ľr where it was appointed for him to receive the Tablets. On the way, he rushed for the appointment with Allah, arriving before the delegation.

20:84.text: He replied, ‚ÄúThey are close on my tracks. And I have hastened to You, my Lord, so You will be pleased.‚ÄĚ

20:85.text: Allah responded, ‚ÄúWe have indeed tested your people in your absence, and the S√Ęmiri<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†has led them astray.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†The S√Ęmiri, or the man from Samaria, was a hypocrite who led the Children of Israel into idol-worship.

20:86.text: So Moses returned to his people, furious and sorrowful. He said, ‚ÄúO my people! Had your Lord not made you a good promise?<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Has my absence been too long for you? Or have you wished for wrath from your Lord to befall you, so you broke your promise to me?‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†
footnotes: ¬†To reveal the Torah for their guidance.
¬†To worship Allah alone until Moses returned with the Tablets.

20:87.text: They argued, ‚ÄúWe did not break our promise to you of our own free will, but we were made to carry the burden of the people‚Äôs ňĻgoldenňļ jewellery,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†then we threw it ňĻinto the fireňļ, and so did the S√Ęmiri.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†The jewellery they borrowed from their Egyptian neighbours before they fled Egypt.

20:88.text: Then he moulded for them an idol of a calf that made a lowing sound. They said, ‚ÄúThis is your god and the god of Moses, but Moses forgot ňĻwhere it wasňļ!‚ÄĚ

20:89.text: Did they not see that it did not respond to them, nor could it protect or benefit them?

20:90.text: Aaron had already warned them beforehand, ‚ÄúO my people! You are only being tested by this, for indeed your ňĻone trueňļ Lord is the Most Compassionate. So follow me and obey my orders.‚ÄĚ

20:91.text: They replied, ‚ÄúWe will not cease to worship it until Moses returns to us.‚ÄĚ

20:92.text: Moses scolded ňĻhis brotherňļ, ‚ÄúO Aaron! What prevented you, when you saw them going astray,

20:93.text: from following after me? How could you disobey my orders?‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Moses‚Äô orders are mentioned in 7:142.

20:94.text: Aaron pleaded, ‚ÄúO son of my mother! Do not seize me by my beard or ňĻthe hair ofňļ my head. I really feared that you would say, ‚ÄėYou have caused division among the Children of Israel, and did not observe my word.‚Äô‚ÄĚ

20:95.text: Moses then asked, ‚ÄúWhat did you think you were doing, O ¬†S√Ęmiri?‚ÄĚ

20:96.text: He said, ‚ÄúI saw what they did not see, so I took a handful ňĻof dustňļ from the hoof-prints of ňĻthe horse ofňļ the messenger-angel ňĻGabrielňļ then cast it ňĻon the moulded calfňļ. This is what my lower-self tempted me into.‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†This verse could also be translated as follows: ‚ÄúI had an insight which they did not have, then grasped some knowledge from the messenger ňĻMosesňļ, but ňĻlaterňļ threw it away. This is what my lower-self tempted me to do.‚ÄĚ According to many Quran commentators, while Moses and the Children of Israel were crossing the sea to escape abuse by Pharaoh and his people, the S√Ęmiri saw Gabriel on a horse leading the way, and every time the horse touched the ground, it turned green. So the S√Ęmiri took a handful of dust from the hoof-prints of the horse, and later tossed it at the calf so it started to make a lowing sound.

20:97.text: Moses said, ‚ÄúGo away then! And for ňĻthe rest of yourňļ life you will surely be crying, ‚ÄėDo not touch ňĻmeňļ!‚Äô<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Then you will certainly have a fate<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>¬†that you cannot escape. Now look at your god to which you have been devoted: we will burn it up, then scatter it in the sea completely.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†Meaning, alienated in the dessert, away from the people.
¬†lit., destined time.

20:98.text: ňĻThen Moses addressed his people,ňļ ‚ÄúYour only god is Allah, there is no god ňĻworthy of worshipňļ except Him. He encompasses everything in ňĻHisňļ knowledge.‚ÄĚ

20:99.text: This is how We relate to you ňĻO Prophetňļ some of the stories of the past. And We have certainly granted you a Reminder<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†from Us.
footnotes: ¬†The Quran.

20:100.text: Whoever turns away from it will surely bear the burden ňĻof sinňļ on the Day of Judgment,

20:101.text: suffering its consequences forever. What an evil burden they will carry on Judgment Day!

20:102.text: ňĻBeware ofňļ the Day the Trumpet will be blown,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†and We will gather the wicked on that Day blue-faced ňĻfrom horror and thirstňļ.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>
footnotes: ¬†See¬†footnote for 18:99.
¬†This can also mean ‚Äúblind.‚ÄĚ

20:103.text: They will whisper among themselves, ‚ÄúYou stayed no more than ten days ňĻon the earthňļ.‚ÄĚ

20:104.text: We know best what they will say‚ÄĒthe most reasonable of them will say, ‚ÄúYou stayed no more than a day.‚ÄĚ

20:105.text: And ňĻifňļ they ask you ňĻO Prophetňļ about the mountains, ňĻthenňļ say, ‚ÄúMy Lord will wipe them out completely,

20:106.text: leaving the earth level and bare,

20:107.text: with neither depressions nor elevations to be seen.‚ÄĚ

20:108.text: On that Day all will follow the caller ňĻfor assemblyňļ, ňĻandňļ none will dare to deviate. All voices will be hushed before the Most Compassionate. Only whispers<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†will be heard.
footnotes: ¬†This can also mean ‚Äúfootsteps.‚ÄĚ

20:109.text: On that Day no intercession will be of any benefit, except by those granted permission by the Most Compassionate and whose words are agreeable to Him.

20:110.text: He ňĻfullyňļ knows what is ahead of them and what is behind them,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†but they cannot encompass Him in ňĻtheirňļ knowledge.<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Allah fully knows what fate awaits them and what they did in the world.
¬†Or ‚Äú‚Ä¶ they cannot encompass His knowledge.‚ÄĚ

20:111.text: And all faces will be humbled before the Ever-Living, All-Sustaining. And those burdened with wrongdoing will be in loss.

20:112.text: But whoever does good and is a believer will have no fear of being wronged or denied ňĻtheir rewardňļ.

20:113.text: And so We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran and varied the warnings in it, so perhaps they will shun evil or it may cause them to be mindful.

20:114.text: Exalted is Allah, the True King! Do not rush to recite ňĻa revelation ofňļ the Quran ňĻO Prophetňļ before it is ňĻproperlyňļ conveyed to you,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†and pray, ‚ÄúMy Lord! Increase me in knowledge.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†The Prophet (Ô∑ļ) was eager to recite the Quran while it was being revealed to him through the angel Gabriel. So he (Ô∑ļ) was told to take his time to learn it by heart once the verses are properly delivered to him.

20:115.text: And indeed, We once made a covenant with Adam, but he forgot, and ňĻsoňļ We did not find determination in him.

20:116.text: And ňĻrememberňļ when We said to the angels, ‚ÄúProstrate before Adam,‚ÄĚ so they all did‚ÄĒbut not Ibl√ģs,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†who refused ňĻarrogantlyňļ.
footnotes: ¬†See¬†footnote for 2:34.

20:117.text: So We cautioned, ‚ÄúO Adam! This is surely an enemy to you and to your wife. So do not let him drive you both out of Paradise, for you ňĻO Adamňļ would then suffer ňĻhardshipňļ.

20:118.text: Here it is guaranteed that you will never go hungry or unclothed,

20:119.text: nor will you ňĻeverňļ suffer from thirst or ňĻthe sun‚Äôsňļ heat.‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†
footnotes: ¬†There is no sun or cold weather in Paradise, only light. See¬†76:13.

20:120.text: But Satan whispered to him, saying, ‚ÄúO Adam! Shall I show you the Tree of Immortality and a kingdom that does not fade away?‚ÄĚ

20:121.text: So they both ate from the tree and then their nakedness was exposed to them, prompting them to cover themselves with leaves from Paradise. So Adam disobeyed his Lord, and ňĻsoňļ lost his way.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†Unlike the Bible (Genesis 3), the Quran does not blame Eve for Adam‚Äôs fall from the Garden.

20:122.text: Then his Lord chose him ňĻfor His graceňļ, accepted his repentance, and guided him ňĻrightlyňļ.

20:123.text: Allah said, ‚ÄúDescend, both of you, from here together ňĻwith Satanňļ as enemies to each other. Then when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance will neither go astray ňĻin this lifeňļ nor suffer ňĻin the nextňļ.

20:124.text: But whoever turns away from My Reminder will certainly have a miserable life,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†then We will raise them up blind on the Day of Judgment.‚ÄĚ
footnotes: ¬†The Reminder is another name for the Quran.

20:125.text: They will cry, ‚ÄúMy Lord! Why have you raised me up blind, although I used to see?‚ÄĚ

20:126.text: Allah will respond, ‚ÄúIt is so, just as Our revelations came to you and you neglected them, so Today you are neglected.‚ÄĚ

20:127.text: This is how We reward whoever transgresses and does not believe in the revelations of their Lord. And the punishment of the Hereafter is far more severe and more lasting.

20:128.text: Is it not yet clear to them how many peoples We destroyed before them, whose ruins they still pass by? Surely in this are signs for people of sound judgment.

20:129.text: Had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†ňĻO Prophetňļ and a term already set, their ňĻinstantňļ doom would have been inevitable.
footnotes: ¬†That He will delay their judgment until the Hereafter.

20:130.text: So be patient ňĻO Prophetňļ with what they say. And glorify the praises of your Lord before sunrise and before sunset, and glorify Him in the hours of the night and at both ends of the day,<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†so that you may be pleased ňĻwith the rewardňļ.
footnotes: ¬†This verse refers to the times of the five daily prayers.

20:131.text: Do not let your eyes crave what We have allowed some of the disbelievers to enjoy; the ňĻfleetingňļ splendour of this worldly life, which We test them with. But your Lord‚Äôs provision ňĻin the Hereafterňļ is far better and more lasting.

20:132.text: Bid your people to pray, and be diligent in ňĻobservingňļ it. We do not ask you to provide. It is We Who provide for you. And the ultimate outcome is ňĻonlyňļ for ňĻthe people ofňļ righteousness.

20:133.text: They demand, ‚ÄúIf only he could bring us a sign from his Lord!‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Have they not ňĻalreadyňļ received a confirmation of what is in earlier Scriptures?<sup foot_note=2>2</sup>
footnotes: ¬†The pagans of Mecca were not satisfied with a literary miracle in the form of the Quran, so they asked for a tangible miracle (see¬†17:90-91) similar to the staff of Moses.

¬†This either refers to the Biblical stories confirmed by the Quran or the prophecies of MuŠł•ammad (Ô∑ļ) in the Bible. Some Muslim scholars cite Deuteronomy 18:15-18 and 33:2, Isaiah 42, and John 14:16 as examples of the description of Prophet MuŠł•ammad (Ô∑ļ) in the Bible. However, Bible scholars interpret these verses differently.

20:134.text: Had We destroyed them with a torment before this ňĻProphet cameňļ, they would have surely argued, ‚ÄúOur Lord! If only You had sent us a messenger, we would have followed Your revelations before being humiliated and put to shame.‚ÄĚ

20:135.text: Say ňĻto them, O ¬†Prophetňļ, ‚ÄúEach ňĻof usňļ is waiting, so keep waiting! You will soon know who is on the Straight Path and is ňĻrightlyňļ guided.‚ÄĚ

21:1.text: ňĻThe time ofňļ people‚Äôs judgment has drawn near, yet they are heedlessly turning away.

21:2.text: Whatever new reminder comes to them from their Lord, they only listen to it jokingly,

21:3.text: with their hearts ňĻtotallyňļ distracted. The evildoers would converse secretly, ňĻsaying,ňļ ‚ÄúIs this ňĻoneňļ not human like yourselves? Would you fall for ňĻthisňļ witchcraft, even though you can ňĻclearlyňļ see?‚ÄĚ

21:4.text: The Prophet responded, ‚ÄúMy Lord ňĻfullyňļ knows every word spoken in the heavens and the earth. For He is the All-Hearing, All-Knowing.‚ÄĚ

21:5.text: Yet they say, ‚ÄúThis ňĻQuranňļ is a set of confused dreams! No, he has fabricated it! No, he must be a poet! So let him bring us a ňĻtangibleňļ sign like those ňĻprophetsňļ sent before.‚ÄĚ

21:6.text: Not a ňĻsingleňļ society We destroyed before them ever believed ňĻafter receiving the signsňļ. Will these ňĻpagansňļ then believe?

21:7.text: We did not send ňĻmessengersňļ before you ňĻO Prophetňļ except mere men inspired by Us. If you ňĻpolytheistsňļ do not know ňĻthis alreadyňļ, then ask those who have knowledge ňĻof the Scripturesňļ.

21:8.text: We did not give those messengers ňĻsupernaturalňļ bodies that did not need food, nor were they immortal.

21:9.text: Then We fulfilled Our promise to them, saving them along with whoever We willed and destroying the transgressors.

21:10.text: We have surely revealed to you a Book, in which there is glory for you. Will you not then understand?

21:11.text: ňĻImagineňļ how many societies of wrongdoers We have destroyed, raising up other people after them!

21:12.text: When the wrongdoers sensed ňĻthe arrival ofňļ Our torment, they started to run away from their cities.

21:13.text: ňĻThey were told,ňļ ‚ÄúDo not run away! Return to your luxuries and your homes, so you may be questioned ňĻabout your fateňļ.‚ÄĚ

21:14.text: They cried, ‚ÄúWoe to us! We have surely been wrongdoers.‚ÄĚ

21:15.text: They kept repeating their cry until We mowed them down, ňĻleaving themňļ lifeless.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†
footnotes: ¬†lit., extinguished like ashes.

21:16.text: We did not create the heavens and the earth and everything in between for sport.

21:17.text: Had We intended to take ňĻsomeňļ amusement, We could have found it in Our presence, if that had been Our Will.

21:18.text: In fact, We hurl the truth against falsehood, leaving it crushed, and it quickly vanishes. And woe be to you for what you claim!<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†That Allah has a partner and children.

21:19.text: To Him belong all those in the heavens and the earth. And those nearest to Him are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they tire.

21:20.text: They glorify ňĻHimňļ day and night, never wavering.

21:21.text: Or have they taken gods from the earth, who can raise the dead?

21:22.text: Had there been other gods besides Allah in the heavens or the earth, both ňĻrealmsňļ would have surely been corrupted. So Glorified is Allah, Lord of the Throne, far above what they claim.

21:23.text: He cannot be questioned about what He does, but they will ňĻallňļ be questioned.

21:24.text: Or have they taken other gods besides Him? Say, ňĻO Prophet,ňļ ‚ÄúShow ňĻmeňļ your proof. Here is ňĻthe Quran,ňļ the Reminder for those with me; along with ňĻearlier Scriptures,ňļ the Reminder for those before me.‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†But most of them do not know the truth, so they turn away.
footnotes: ¬†The Prophet (Ô∑ļ) is ordered to challenge the pagans to produce logical and scriptural proofs for the existence of other gods beside Allah. The verses then argue that if there is more than one god, the different gods would have fought among themselves for domination, and the whole universe would have been ruined. The Quran, the Torah, and the Gospel are all in agreement that there is only One God.

21:25.text: We never sent a messenger before you ňĻO Prophetňļ without revealing to him: ‚ÄúThere is no god ňĻworthy of worshipňļ except Me, so worship Me ňĻaloneňļ.‚ÄĚ

21:26.text: And they say, ‚ÄúThe Most Compassionate has offspring!‚ÄĚ<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>¬†Glory be to Him! In fact, those ňĻangelsňļ are only ňĻHisňļ honoured servants,
footnotes: ¬†Some pagans claimed that the angels are Allah‚Äôs daughters.

21:27.text: who do not speak until He has spoken, ňĻonlyňļ acting at His command.

21:28.text: He ňĻfullyňļ knows what is ahead of them and what is behind them. They do not intercede except for whom He approves, and they tremble in awe of Him.

21:29.text: Whoever of them were to say, ‚ÄúI am a god besides Him,‚ÄĚ they would be rewarded with Hell by Us. This is how We reward the wrongdoers.

21:30.text: Do the disbelievers not realize that the heavens and earth were ňĻonceňļ one mass then We split them apart? And We created from water every living thing. Will they not then believe?

21:31.text: And We have placed firm mountains upon the earth so it does not shake with them, and made in it broad pathways so they may find their way.

21:32.text: And We have made the sky a well-protected canopy, still they turn away from its signs.<sup foot_note=1>1</sup>
footnotes: ¬†The signs of the heaven include the galaxies, planets, stars, etc., all of which have an orbit. Our planet orbits the sun, the sun orbits the Milky Way Galaxy, and the Milky Way Galaxy orbits the Virgo Supercluster, etc.

21:33.text: And He is the One Who created the day and the night, the sun and the moon‚ÄĒeach travelling in an orbit.

21:34.text: We have not granted immortality to any human before you ňĻO Prophetňļ: so if you die, will they live forever?

21:35.text: Every soul will taste death. And We test you ňĻO humanityňļ with good and evil as a trial, then to Us you will ňĻallňļ be returned.
